name.text: Week 13 – Q3: Reflection Coefficient, VSWR, and Standing Wave Locations

questiontext.text:
Reflection Coefficient, VSWR, and Standing Wave Locations

A lossless transmission line with characteristic impedance \(Z_0 = {@Z0_disp@}\;\Omega\) is terminated by a load impedance \(Z_L = {@RL_disp@} {@if is(XL >= 0) then "+" else "-"@} j{@abs(XL_disp)@}\;\Omega\). The operating wavelength is \(\lambda = {@lambda_disp@}\;\text{cm}\).

Compute the reflection coefficient, VSWR, and locations of the first voltage maximum and minimum from the load.

Part (a) — Magnitude of the reflection coefficient (1 pt)

Compute \(|\Gamma|\).

\(|\Gamma| = \) [[input:ans1]] [[validation:ans1]]

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 0.447

Part (b) — Phase of the reflection coefficient (1 pt)

Compute the phase angle \(\theta_\Gamma\) in degrees.

\(\theta_\Gamma = \) [[input:ans2]] [[validation:ans2]] °

Syntax hint: Enter the angle in degrees, e.g. -26.57

Part (c) — VSWR (1 pt)

Compute the voltage standing wave ratio (VSWR).

VSWR \(= \) [[input:ans3]] [[validation:ans3]]

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 2.618

Part (d) — First voltage maximum distance from load (1 pt)

Find the distance of the first voltage maximum from the load, \(d_{\max}\), in metres.

\(d_{\max} = \) [[input:ans4]] [[validation:ans4]] m

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 0.0382

Part (e) — First voltage minimum distance from load (1 pt)

Find the distance of the first voltage minimum from the load, \(d_{\min}\), in metres.

\(d_{\min} = \) [[input:ans5]] [[validation:ans5]] m

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 0.1582

Part (f) — Matched load reflection coefficient (1 pt)

If the load impedance equals the characteristic impedance (\(Z_L = Z_0\)), what is \(|\Gamma|\)?

[[input:ans6]] [[validation:ans6]]

Syntax hint: Select the correct value from the dropdown.

Part (g) — Physical meaning of VSWR = 1 (1 pt)

What does VSWR = 1 mean physically?

[[input:ans7]] [[validation:ans7]]

Syntax hint: Select the most physically accurate explanation.

Part (h) — Design constraint: maximum load resistance for VSWR ≤ 2 (1 pt)

In parts (a)–(e) you analysed a complex load. Now consider a simplified design scenario: a purely resistive load \(R_L\) (no reactance) is connected to a transmission line with characteristic impedance \(Z_0 = {@Z0_disp@}\;\Omega\). This simplification is common in initial design stages before fine-tuning with matching networks.

What is the maximum value of \(R_L\) (assuming \(R_L > Z_0\)) that keeps \(\text{VSWR} \le 2\)?

\(R_{L,\max} = \) [[input:ans8]] [[validation:ans8]] Ω

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 100

Part (i) — Connecting back: does the complex load meet the VSWR constraint? (1 pt)

You found that the maximum purely resistive \(R_L\) for VSWR \(\le 2\) is \({@ta8@}\;\Omega\).

Now look back at your results from parts (a)–(c): the complex load \(Z_L = {@RL_disp@} {@if is(XL >= 0) then "+" else "-"@} j{@abs(XL_disp)@}\;\Omega\) gave \(|\Gamma| = {@ta1@}\) and \(\text{VSWR} = {@ta3_disp@}\).

Does this complex load satisfy the VSWR \(\le 2\) constraint?

[[input:ans9]] [[validation:ans9]]

Syntax hint: Select the statement that correctly uses your earlier results.

Part (j) — Why the resistive formula is not enough (1 pt)

Explain in 1–2 sentences: why does the purely resistive formula \(R_L \le 2 Z_0\) not guarantee VSWR \(\le 2\) when the load has a reactive component \(jX_L\)? What role does \(X_L\) play?

[[input:ans10]] [[validation:ans10]]

Syntax hint: Write a brief explanation (1–2 sentences). This part is graded for completion; a model answer is provided after submission.

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

Part (a) — Magnitude of the reflection coefficient

The reflection coefficient is:

\[\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} = \frac{({@RL_disp@} {@if is(XL >= 0) then "+" else "-"@} j{@abs(XL_disp)@}) - {@Z0_disp@}}{({@RL_disp@} {@if is(XL >= 0) then "+" else "-"@} j{@abs(XL_disp)@}) + {@Z0_disp@}}\]

Its magnitude is:

\[|\Gamma| = {@ta1@}\]

Part (b) — Phase of the reflection coefficient

The phase angle (argument) of the complex reflection coefficient is:

\[\theta_\Gamma = {@ta2@}^\circ\]

Part (c) — VSWR

The voltage standing wave ratio is:

\[\text{VSWR} = \frac{1 + |\Gamma|}{1 - |\Gamma|} = \frac{1 + {@ta1@}}{1 - {@ta1@}} = {@ta3@}\]

Part (d) — First voltage maximum

The first voltage maximum occurs at a distance from the load:

\[d_{\max} = \frac{\theta_\Gamma}{2\beta}\]

where \(\beta = 2\pi/\lambda\). If the result is negative, add \(\lambda/2\).

\[d_{\max} = {@ta4@}\;\text{m}\]

Part (e) — First voltage minimum

The first voltage minimum is separated from the first maximum by \(\lambda/4\):

\[d_{\min} = d_{\max} + \frac{\lambda}{4} \pmod{\lambda/2}\]

If the result exceeds \(\lambda/2\), subtract \(\lambda/2\) to get the first occurrence.

\[d_{\min} = {@ta5@}\;\text{m}\]

Part (f) — Matched load

When \(Z_L = Z_0\), the numerator \(Z_L - Z_0 = 0\), so \(|\Gamma| = 0\). There is no reflection — all power is absorbed by the load.

Part (g) — VSWR = 1

VSWR = 1 means \(|\Gamma| = 0\): the line is perfectly matched, no reflections occur, and all incident power is delivered to the load. The voltage amplitude is constant along the line.

Part (h) — Maximum load resistance for VSWR ≤ 2

For a purely resistive load with \(R_L > Z_0\):

\[\text{VSWR} = \frac{R_L}{Z_0} \le 2 \implies R_L \le 2 Z_0 = 2 \times {@Z0_disp@} = {@ta8@}\;\Omega\]

Equivalently, from \(|\Gamma| = (R_L - Z_0)/(R_L + Z_0) \le 1/3\), solving gives \(R_L \le 2 Z_0\).

Part (i) — Complex load vs. VSWR constraint

The VSWR of the complex load was computed in part (c): VSWR \(= {@ta3@}\).

{@if is(ta3 <= 2.0) then "Since VSWR = " else "Since VSWR = "@}{@ta3_disp@}{@if is(ta3 <= 2.0) then " ≤ 2, the constraint is satisfied." else " > 2, the constraint is not satisfied — a matching network would be needed."@}

Note: the resistive formula \(R_L \le 2 Z_0\) cannot be applied to a complex load. The full VSWR must be computed from \(|\Gamma|\).

Part (j) — Role of reactance

The formula \(R_L \le 2 Z_0\) assumes \(X_L = 0\). When \(X_L \ne 0\), the reflection coefficient magnitude depends on both \(R_L\) and \(X_L\):

\[|\Gamma| = \frac{|Z_L - Z_0|}{|Z_L + Z_0|} = \frac{\sqrt{(R_L - Z_0)^2 + X_L^2}}{\sqrt{(R_L + Z_0)^2 + X_L^2}}\]

The reactive component \(X_L\) increases the numerator more than the denominator, which increases \(|\Gamma|\) and therefore VSWR. A load with \(R_L < 2 Z_0\) can still have VSWR \(> 2\) if \(|X_L|\) is large enough.

Answer Summary

PartAnswer
(a) |Γ|{@ta1@}
(b) θΓ{@ta2@}°
(c) VSWR{@ta3@}
(d) dmax{@ta4@} m
(e) dmin{@ta5@} m
(f) |Γ| when ZL=Z00
(g) VSWR=1 meaningPerfectly matched — no reflections
(h) RL,max for VSWR≤2{@ta8@} Ω
(i) Complex load VSWR check{@if is(ta3 <= 2.0) then "Yes — VSWR ≤ 2" else "No — VSWR > 2"@}
(j) Role of reactanceSee model answer above

specificfeedback.text:
Part (a) — |Γ|

[[feedback:prt1]]

Part (b) — θΓ

[[feedback:prt2]]

Part (c) — VSWR

[[feedback:prt3]]

Part (d) — First voltage maximum

[[feedback:prt4]]

Part (e) — First voltage minimum

[[feedback:prt5]]

Part (f) — Matched load |Γ|

[[feedback:prt6]]

Part (g) — VSWR = 1 meaning

[[feedback:prt7]]

Part (h) — Max RL for VSWR ≤ 2

[[feedback:prt8]]

Part (i) — Complex load VSWR check

[[feedback:prt9]]

Part (j) — Role of reactance

[[feedback:prt10]]

questionnote.text: Set {@set@}: Z0={@Z0_disp@} Ω, ZL={@RL_disp@}+j{@XL_disp@} Ω, λ={@lambda_disp@} cm | |Γ|={@ta1@}, θ={@ta2@}°, VSWR={@ta3@}, dmax={@ta4@} m, dmin={@ta5@} m | RL_max={@ta8@} | VSWR_check={@if is(ta3 <= 2.0) then "Yes" else "No"@}

questiondescription.text: Reflection coefficient, VSWR, and standing wave locations — Week 13 training exercise.

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done!

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.

input.name: ans10
input.syntaxhint: Think about how X_L affects the numerator and denominator of |Gamma|.

truefeedback.text: Correct! The reflection coefficient magnitude is \(|\Gamma| = {@ta1@}\). This tells us that {@decimalplaces(ta1*100, 1)@}% of the incident voltage wave is reflected back toward the source.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The reflection coefficient is \(\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)\). With \(Z_L = {@RL_disp@} + j({@XL_disp@})\;\Omega\) and \(Z_0 = {@Z0_disp@}\;\Omega\), compute the complex ratio and take its magnitude. The correct answer is \(|\Gamma| = {@ta1@}\).

truefeedback.text: Correct! The phase angle of the reflection coefficient is \(\theta_\Gamma = {@ta2@}^\circ\). This phase determines the positions of voltage maxima and minima along the line.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Compute \(\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)\) as a complex number and find its argument (angle) in degrees. The correct answer is \(\theta_\Gamma = {@ta2@}^\circ\).

truefeedback.text: Correct! VSWR \(= {@ta3@}\). This means the ratio of maximum to minimum voltage amplitude on the line is {@ta3@}:1. A VSWR of 1 would indicate a perfect match.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. VSWR is computed from the reflection coefficient magnitude: \(\text{VSWR} = (1 + |\Gamma|)/(1 - |\Gamma|) = (1 + {@ta1@})/(1 - {@ta1@}) = {@ta3@}\).

node.description: Check part (d): first voltage maximum distance

truefeedback.text: Correct! The first voltage maximum is at \(d_{\max} = {@ta4@}\;\text{m}\) from the load. Voltage maxima occur where the incident and reflected waves add constructively.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The first voltage maximum occurs at \(d_{\max} = \theta_\Gamma / (2\beta)\), where \(\beta = 2\pi/\lambda\). If this value is negative, add \(\lambda/2\) to obtain the first positive distance. The correct answer is \(d_{\max} = {@ta4@}\;\text{m}\).

node.description: Check part (e): first voltage minimum distance

truefeedback.text: Correct! The first voltage minimum is at \(d_{\min} = {@ta5@}\;\text{m}\) from the load. Voltage minima occur where the incident and reflected waves interfere destructively, and are always separated from maxima by \(\lambda/4\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The first voltage minimum is at \(d_{\min} = d_{\max} + \lambda/4\), reduced modulo \(\lambda/2\) if it exceeds \(\lambda/2\). The correct answer is \(d_{\min} = {@ta5@}\;\text{m}\).

truefeedback.text: Correct! When \(Z_L = Z_0\), \(\Gamma = (Z_0 - Z_0)/(Z_0 + Z_0) = 0\). A matched load absorbs all incident power with no reflection.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. When \(Z_L = Z_0\), the numerator of \(\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)\) is zero, so \(|\Gamma| = 0\). This is the matched condition — no reflected wave exists.

truefeedback.text: Correct! VSWR = 1 means \(|\Gamma| = 0\): the transmission line is perfectly matched to its load. There are no reflections, and the voltage amplitude is uniform along the entire line. All incident power is delivered to the load.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. VSWR = 1 corresponds to \(|\Gamma| = 0\), which means the line is perfectly matched — no reflections occur and all incident power is delivered to the load. The voltage envelope is constant along the line (no standing wave pattern).

node.description: Check part (h): max RL for VSWR <= 2

truefeedback.text: Correct! The maximum purely resistive load for VSWR ≤ 2 is \(R_{L,\max} = 2 Z_0 = {@ta8@}\;\Omega\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. For a purely resistive load with \(R_L > Z_0\), VSWR \(= R_L / Z_0\). Setting VSWR \(\le 2\) gives \(R_L \le 2 Z_0 = {@ta8@}\;\Omega\).

node.description: Check part (i): complex load VSWR check

truefeedback.text: Correct! The complex load's VSWR is {@ta3_disp@}, which is {@if is(ta3 <= 2.0) then "≤ 2 — the constraint is met" else "> 2 — a matching network is needed"@}. The key insight is that you must use the actual VSWR from the complex impedance, not the simplified resistive formula.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Look back at your VSWR from part (c): VSWR \(= {@ta3_disp@}\). Compare this directly to 2. The resistive formula \(R_L \le 2 Z_0\) cannot be applied to complex loads — you must use the actual VSWR computed from \(|\Gamma|\).

truefeedback.text: Thank you for your explanation. Compare your reasoning with the model answer in the worked solution above.

falsefeedback.text: Thank you for your explanation. Compare your reasoning with the model answer in the worked solution above.

hint.text: Hint 1: The reflection coefficient \(\Gamma\) measures the mismatch between the load impedance and the characteristic impedance. Start with \(\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)\). Since \(Z_L\) is complex, \(\Gamma\) will also be complex — you need both its magnitude and phase.

Hint 2: To compute \(|\Gamma|\), find the magnitude of the complex ratio. For the phase, use the argument (angle) of the complex number and convert to degrees. VSWR follows directly: \(\text{VSWR} = (1 + |\Gamma|)/(1 - |\Gamma|)\). The phase constant is \(\beta = 2\pi/\lambda\).

Hint 3: The first voltage maximum occurs at \(d_{\max} = \theta_\Gamma/(2\beta)\) from the load (if \(\theta_\Gamma > 0\); otherwise add \(\lambda/2\)). The first voltage minimum is at \(d_{\min} = d_{\max} + \lambda/4\), reduced by \(\lambda/2\) if it exceeds \(\lambda/2\). Maxima and minima always alternate every \(\lambda/4\) along the line.

For part (h), start from the VSWR definition for a purely resistive load: VSWR \(= R_L/Z_0\) when \(R_L > Z_0\). Set VSWR \(\le 2\) and solve for \(R_L\).

For part (i), compare the VSWR you calculated in part (c) directly to the threshold of 2. Don't try to use the resistive formula from part (h) — it only applies when \(X_L = 0\).

For part (j), think about how \(X_L\) appears in the formula \(|\Gamma| = |Z_L - Z_0| / |Z_L + Z_0|\). The numerator and denominator both contain \(X_L\), but which one grows faster?
